passage: ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib realized that his own death would not be long in coming. The future of the orphan child was one of his most immediate concerns. He therefore called in his son Abū Ṭālib and asked him specifically to look after Muhammad, his nephew, when he himself had died. It was good that he did so, because ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib died within two years of Muhammad coming into his care. It is said that ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib was a hundred and twenty when he died, but his grandchild was only eight. Again, death snatched away a loving soul from Muhammad’s life. He was extremely distressed to lose his grandfather. He felt he had lost the man whose kindness to him could not be equalled by any other. He grieved for his loss as only a loving child could grieve when he realized that he would not be seeing his beloved one any more. Perhaps ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib chose to trust Muhammad to the care of Abū Ṭālib because the latter had the same mother as ʿ Abdullāh, Muhammad’s father. He might also have realized that Abū Ṭālib was the kindest and most caring of his children. This explains why Abū Ṭālib was chosen for this task, despite the fact that he had many children of his own and was a man of little means. Many of Muhammad’s other uncles were better placed to look after him, from a financial point of view. Yet ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib chose Abū Ṭālib, and what an appropriate choice it proved to be. Abū Ṭālib continued to look after Muhammad until he became a man. Even then, he continued to show him the loving care a father shows to his adult son. He was never slow in giving him sound advice and guidance. When Muhammad started to receive his message and convey it to people, Abū Ṭālib supported him in the face of strong opposition from the Quraysh. He never failed him even when the pressures were too strong to bear for an old man, as Abū Ṭālib was at that time. There was a relationship of mutual love and respect between uncle and orphan nephew. Indeed, Abū Ṭālib loved Muhammad as much as he loved his dearest child, if not more. Again when he was in the care of his uncle, there were signs that God’s blessings were associated with the presence of Muhammad. While there was no sudden influx of riches into the house of Abū Ṭālib, there always seemed to be enough when Muhammad was there. If dinner was served and Muhammad was not present, Abū Ṭālib would order his children to wait for him. He had noticed that when Muhammad was eating with them, the food seemed plentiful and everybody had his fill. If he was absent, the food seemed not to be sufficient and everybody asked for more. On the whole, Muhammad’s childhood was very pleasant. He radiated happiness to all around him. Hence it was not surprising that he was loved dearly by all those close to him. 15 We have several reports of that period in the life of Muhammad which suggest that various people recognized him as the future Prophet. Many suggest that the people recognizing him tried to get him killed. The first reports speak of Ḥalīmah taking Muhammad to a fortune-teller to divine his future. None of these reports, however, attains a sufficient degree of authenticity to make it of any great value. Such things might have happened. Their effect either on the boy himself or on those looking after him was limited indeed. Nor can the story of Muhammad’s encounter with the Christian monk of the town of Buṣrāin southern Syria be of great importance. This story suggests that Muhammad clung to his uncle, Abū Ṭālib, when he was about to depart on a trade journey to Syria, and would not let him go without him. Abū Ṭālib then decided to take his twelve-year- old nephew with him. It is said that on their way back home after finishing their business in Syria, this monk, Baḥīrā , invited the whole caravan to a dinner. This was a marked departure from his past habit. He insisted that everybody in the party should attend. He recognized Muhammad and spoke to him, questioning him on many aspects of his life. He also recognized a mark on Muhammad’s shoulder which indicated that he was to be the last Prophet. When he was certain of that fact, Baḥīrāasked Abū Ṭālib what relation the boy was to him. When Abū Ṭālib said that he was his son – as the Arabs considered that an uncle was in the same position as a father – Baḥīrāsaid: “He is not your son. This boy’s father should not be alive.” Abū Ṭālib told him that Muhammad was his nephew and that his father had died before his birth. Baḥīrāsaid: “That is right. Take your nephew back to his home town, and watch him carefully. Should the Jews recognize him as I have done they would try to harm him. This nephew of yours is certain to have a great future.” 16 Whatever the truth about this story and the other reports to which we have referred, it is certain that they did not influence Muhammad in any way. We have to remember that he was still a child, and he could not have aspired to any distinction as a result of Baḥīrā ’s discourse. Moreover, it seems that the men who heard Baḥīrā ’s conversation with Abū Ṭālib did not bother to relate it to other people. The only value of these reports is that they confirm the fact that learned men of other religions were aware of the imminent appearance of a Prophet in Arabia. Their knowledge is based on what is definitely contained in their scriptures. Muhammad was only twelve when he went on this trip with his uncle, Abū Ṭālib. Some reports suggest that he was even younger. Not long after he came back to Makkah he realized that he had to do something in order to help his poor uncle, who had a large family to support. Although from the time of his birth Muhammad brought his blessings to his immediate environment, wherever it happened to be – as clearly related by Ḥalīmah, his wet nurse – he was not meant to enjoy a life of affluence. There always seemed to be enough for everyone around, but there was little to spare. Muhammad himself needed very little: he was content with whatever was available to him. But he always had a keen sense of what was going on around him. His uncle’s situation cried out for help, and Muhammad was aware of that.
source: Adil Salahi, Muhammad: Man and Prophet, p. 32

story_context.introduction: Imagine being a young boy, living with your loving uncle after your grandfather has passed away. This is the story of Muhammad, a boy who would grow up to be a great Prophet. His uncle, Abu Talib, took care of him and showed him kindness and love.
story_context.conclusion: Muhammad was grateful for his uncle's love and care. He knew that he was lucky to have someone like Abu Talib in his life. And as we can see, Abu Talib's love and care for Muhammad had a special impact on those around them.
story_context.moral_lesson: The importance of kindness and love in our relationships with others. Just like Abu Talib showed love and care to Muhammad, we can show love and care to those around us, making a positive impact on their lives.

units.unit_title: Abd al-Muṭṭalib's Concern for Muhammad's Future
units.unit_text: ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib realized that his own death would not be long in coming. The future of the orphan child was one of his most immediate concerns. He therefore called in his son Abū Ṭālib and asked him specifically to look after Muhammad, his nephew, when he himself had died.
units.event_summary: ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib asks Abū Ṭālib to care for Muhammad after his death.

units.unit_title: Muhammad's Grief over ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib's Death
units.unit_text: It is said that ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib was a hundred and twenty when he died, but his grandchild was only eight. Again, death snatched away a loving soul from Muhammad’s life. He was extremely distressed to lose his grandfather.
units.event_summary: Muhammad mourns the death of his grandfather, ʿ Abd al-Muṭṭalib.

units.unit_text: Abū Ṭālib continued to look after Muhammad until he became a man. Even then, he continued to show him the loving care a father shows to his adult son.
units.event_summary: Abū Ṭālib provides for Muhammad's needs and offers guidance as he grows up.

units.unit_title: Abū Ṭālib's Support for Muhammad's Message
units.unit_text: When Muhammad started to receive his message and convey it to people, Abū Ṭālib supported him in the face of strong opposition from the Quraysh.
units.event_summary: Abū Ṭālib stands by Muhammad as he begins to share his message with others.

units.unit_title: Signs of God's Blessings in Muhammad's Presence
units.unit_text: While there was no sudden influx of riches into the house of Abū Ṭālib, there always seemed to be enough when Muhammad was there.
units.event_summary: Muhammad's presence seems to bring blessings and abundance to Abū Ṭālib's household.

units.unit_text: The first reports speak of Ḥalīmah taking Muhammad to a fortune-teller to divine his future.
units.event_summary: Ḥalīmah takes Muhammad to a fortune-teller, but the outcome is unclear.

units.unit_title: Muhammad's Encounter with the Christian Monk Baḥīrā
units.unit_text: Abū Ṭālib then decided to take his twelve-year-old nephew with him to Syria. On their way back home after finishing their business in Syria, this monk, Baḥīrā, invited the whole caravan to a dinner.
units.event_summary: Abū Ṭālib takes Muhammad to Syria, where they meet the Christian monk Baḥīrā.

units.unit_title: Baḥīrā's Recognition of Muhammad's Prophetic Status
units.unit_text: Baḥīrā recognized Muhammad and spoke to him, questioning him on many aspects of his life. He also recognized a mark on Muhammad’s shoulder which indicated that he was to be the last Prophet.
units.event_summary: Baḥīrā recognizes Muhammad as the last Prophet and offers guidance.

units.unit_title: Muhammad's Sense of Responsibility towards Abū Ṭālib
units.unit_text: Not long after he came back to Makkah he realized that he had to do something in order to help his poor uncle, who had a large family to support.
units.event_summary: Muhammad feels a sense of responsibility towards his uncle, Abū Ṭālib.

scene_input.scene_summary: Abd al-Muṭṭalib reflects on his mortality
scene_input.key_visual_action: Abd al-Muṭṭalib sitting on a bed, looking worried

panels.narrative_text: Abd al-Muṭṭalib sat on his bed, feeling worried about his future. He thought about his mortality and what would happen to him after he died.
panels.moral_lesson: The importance of being prepared for one's death and the afterlife
panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor storybook illustration in soft, warm hand-painted style for ages 6-12. Depict Abd al-Muttalib, the central figure of Islam's early history, sitting on a simple bed in a modestly furnished room, looking worried. The room is lit by a small window with a warm yellow glow, casting a gentle light on Abd al-Muttalib's concerned face.

Abd al-Muttalib, a tall and slender man, sits on the edge of the bed with his back straight, his weight shifted slightly to one side. He wears a simple white thobe, the fabric loose and flowing around his lean frame, and a brown woolen cloak draped over his shoulders. A white cloth head covering, tied at the back of his head, completes his modest attire.

The room is small and unadorned, with minimal furnishings. A low table stands in one corner, bearing a small oil lamp, its glass container reflecting the warm yellow light from the window. A simple rug covers the floor, its soft blue pattern a soothing contrast to the warm yellows and browns that dominate the scene. A few sparse, fragrant dates lie scattered on a nearby tray, their sweet aroma wafting through the air.

As Abd al-Muttalib sits, lost in thought, his slender hands are clasped together in front of him, his knuckles white with tension. His worried expression is palpable, his furrowed brow and pursed lips betraying his concern. The warm yellow light from the window casts a golden glow on his face, highlighting his features and emphasizing his distress.

The composition is a close-up, shot from directly in front of Abd al-Muttalib, capturing his anxious expression and worried demeanor. The soft blue and warm yellow color palette creates a soothing atmosphere, while the simple furnishings and sparse decor evoke a sense of humility and modesty.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

characters.name: Abd al-Muttalib
characters.appearance: tall, slender build, wearing a white thobe with a brown woolen cloak and a white cloth head covering, sitting on a simple bed in a modestly furnished room

compliance.notes: No graphic violence, no idols in detail, historically accurate 7th century scene

full_schema.source_reference: Adil Salahi, Muhammad: Man and Prophet, p. 65

panels.image_prompt:
Illustration: A heartwarming and intimate scene in a traditional 7th century Arabian home, radiating a warm and cozy ambiance. The soft warm hand-painted watercolor style will transport young readers to a bygone era. The scene is set in a modest mud-brick dwelling, with a simple earthenware jug and a woven reed mat adding to the warm and inviting atmosphere.

In the center of the illustration, the man in the green cloak stands upright, his dignified presence commanding attention. His flowing white thobe and deep green woolen cloak, draped elegantly over both shoulders, exude a sense of wisdom and authority. A white turban adorns his head, with a cloth tail hanging between the shoulder blades, adding a touch of elegance to his overall demeanor.

To his left, Abu Talib stands, slightly stooped but still upright, his simple white thobe and plain brown woolen cloak a testament to his humble nature. A white cloth head covering wraps around his forehead, adding a subtle touch of modesty to his appearance. His eyes are cast downward, his expression a picture of concern, as if he is deeply troubled by the conversation unfolding before him.

The central figure, the man in the green cloak, gently points to a nearby object, his expression a mix of seriousness and compassion. His gesture is a subtle yet powerful reminder of the importance of the moment.

The background of the illustration is a warm and inviting space, with a soft light emanating from a nearby candle or lantern. The dominant color palette is warm earth tones, with accents of deep green and white adding a touch of elegance and sophistication. The overall effect is a sense of warmth, comfort, and deep respect for the individuals involved.

Composition-wise, the illustration is shot from a slight angle, with the central figure positioned at the forefront. The camera angle adds a sense of intimacy and immediacy to the scene, drawing the viewer into the heart of the conversation.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor illustration in a soft warm hand-painted style for a children's storybook, ages 6-12. The scene is set in the simple yet cozy home of the central figure, seen from behind, in a warm 7th century Arabian setting. A few furnishings and objects adorn the space, reminiscent of the era - a low wooden table in the center, a woven reed mat covering the floor, and a few intricately patterned cushions against the wall.

The central figure, a man in the green cloak, sits on a cushion, his medium build and dignified upright posture exuding a sense of calm. He wears a flowing white thobe, its folds elegantly draped around his body, and a plain brown woolen cloak draped over his shoulders. A white turban wraps around his head, with a cloth tail hanging between his shoulder blades, adding a touch of traditional elegance. One hand extends from his lap, his fingers splayed in a gentle reassuring gesture, as if comforting himself or someone else.

The room is bathed in a warm soft light, with a subtle hint of sadness in the color palette. The light source emanates from a small window, high up on the wall, casting a gentle glow over the space. The quality of the light is soft and diffused, with a slight warmth that seems to emanate from the very walls themselves. The color palette is muted, with a focus on earthy tones - sandy beiges, soft grays, and muted blues - which add to the sense of sadness and contemplation.

The composition is a medium shot, with the central figure placed slightly off-center, drawing the viewer's eye to his gentle gesture. The background is soft and blurred, with the focus firmly on the central figure and his emotional state. The overall mood of the illustration is one of quiet contemplation and sadness, inviting the viewer to reflect on the central figure's emotional journey.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor storybook illustration, soft warm hand-painted style for ages 6-12: In a serene 7th century Arabian domestic space, a quiet moment of devotion unfolds. The central figure, the Man in the Green Cloak, stands majestically from behind, his dignified posture conveying a sense of reverence. He is draped in a flowing white thobe, its gentle folds a testament to the simplicity of his attire. Over his shoulders, a deep green woolen cloak is draped, its rich color accentuating the soft blues and whites that dominate the scene.

The warm golden light emanating from a small lamp or candle casts a gentle glow on the space, illuminating the intricate patterns of the thobe and the soft folds of the cloak. This soft light also highlights the Man in the Green Cloak's hands, clasped together in a gesture of supplication, as if in heartfelt prayer. His one extended hand, with its gentle reassuring gesture, conveys a sense of comfort and solace.

In the background, a few simple furnishings - a low table, a few cushions, and a door or window - blend seamlessly into the warm, golden light, creating a sense of intimacy and tranquility. The overall composition is a gentle, serene shot from behind the Man in the Green Cloak, with the door or window framing his figure and leading the viewer's eye towards the soft, muted colors of the scene.

The color palette, predominantly soft blues and whites, is punctuated by the deep green of the cloak, creating a sense of balance and harmony. The overall mood is one of peaceful contemplation, inviting the viewer to step into the serene world of this 7th century domestic space.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor illustration of a quiet room in a family member's home, with a soft warm hand-painted style for ages 6-12. The scene is set in a simple, yet elegant, 7th century Arabian home, with a focus on a central wooden table and two chairs. The walls are adorned with intricate patterns and colors, typical of the time period.

In the center of the room, the man in the green cloak sits with his back straight, hands clasped together in a gesture of concern. His dignified upright posture commands attention, as he sits in contemplation. His flowing white thobe is perfectly draped, with the deep green woolen cloak draped elegantly over both shoulders. A white turban sits atop his head, with a cloth tail hanging between his shoulder blades, adding to the overall sense of dignity and wisdom.

Across from him, a young woman sits in a similar chair, her posture a mirror of his. She gazes at the central figure with a serious expression, her eyes conveying a sense of concern and empathy. Her flowing jilbab is a soft, creamy white, with intricate patterns embroidered along the hem. A khimar covers her hair, adding to her modesty and elegance.

The room is bathed in a soft warm glow, with a hint of sunlight streaming in through the window. The light casts a comforting warmth on the scene, creating a sense of tranquility and peace. The dominant color of the scene is a soothing light blue, with accents of deep green and white adding depth and texture. The soft, watercolor brushstrokes and warm color palette create a sense of intimacy and closeness, drawing the viewer into the quiet moment between the two figures.

The composition is a close-up, shot from slightly above, emphasizing the connection between the two figures. The negative space around them creates a sense of calm and serenity, making the viewer feel like they are part of the quiet moment.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

scene_input.scene_summary: Abū Ṭālib stands by Muhammad despite the opposition.
scene_input.key_visual_action: Abū Ṭālib and Muhammad standing together, looking brave.

panels.narrative_text: Abu Talib stood by Muhammad's side, even when faced with opposition from their community.
panels.moral_lesson: Standing up for one's beliefs and community
panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor storybook illustration, soft warm hand-painted style for ages 6-12. In the bustling public square of Mecca, Abu Talib and the central figure seen from behind stand together, exuding an air of bravery and unity. Abu Talib, with his medium build and dignified upright posture, commands attention in his flowing white thobe, its edges slightly frayed from years of wear. His dignified demeanor is emphasized by the deep green woolen cloak draped over both shoulders, its vibrant hue a striking contrast to the desert surroundings.

In the background, the crowd is a gentle blur, their faces seen from behind as they go about their daily business, their features indistinct yet their numbers evident. The sky above is a brilliant blue, with a few puffy white clouds drifting lazily across it, their soft peaks a gentle echo of the peaceful atmosphere.

Muhammad, standing tall and proud behind Abu Talib, is a picture of calm resolve, his medium build and dignified posture radiating an aura of quiet strength. His flowing white thobe billows gently in the breeze, while his plain brown woolen cloak is draped neatly over his shoulders, its earthy tones blending harmoniously with the desert landscape.

The dominant color of the scene is the deep green of Abu Talib's cloak, which draws the eye and imbues the illustration with a sense of energy and vitality. Accents of white and brown add depth and nuance to the composition, while the soft, warm hand-painted style imbues the scene with a sense of timelessness and authenticity.

As the two men stand together, their bond and solidarity are palpable, a testament to the enduring power of friendship and loyalty in the face of adversity. The scene is set against the backdrop of ancient Mecca, with its simple yet elegant architecture, its bustling markets, and its vibrant, multicultural community. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.unit_title: Signs of God's Blessings in Muhammad's Presence

scene_input.scene_summary: Food appears out of nowhere, filling the plates and bowls.

panels.narrative_text: Abu Talib was amazed as food suddenly appeared on the table, filling their plates and bowls. Muhammad smiled, knowing that Allah had provided for them.
panels.moral_lesson: Trust in Allah's provisions and blessings.
panels.image_prompt:
Illustration Prompt: A Heartwarming Moment of Blessings

In a cozy domestic setting, reminiscent of 7th century Arabia, Abu Talib and the central figure seen from behind sit at a simple wooden table, surrounded by earthenware plates and bowls adorned with intricate patterns. The room is filled with warm natural light streaming through a small window, casting a gentle glow on the scene. The soft, honey-colored light illuminates the dust motes dancing in the air, adding to the sense of serenity.

Abu Talib, with his medium build and dignified upright posture, sits with his back straight, exuding a sense of calm and authority. He wears a flowing white thobe, its fabric slightly rumpled, and a deep green woolen cloak draped over both shoulders, its hem just above the floor. His head is covered with a white khuffiya, adding to his air of quiet wisdom.

The central figure seen from behind, with his slender build and dignified posture, sits beside Abu Talib, his deep green woolen cloak draped over both shoulders, mirroring Abu Talib's attire. His white thobe rustles softly, and his head is covered with a matching white khuffiya.

In the center of the table, a miraculous spread of food materializes, filling the plates and bowls. The food is depicted in a simple, yet appealing way, with a variety of colors and textures. Juicy dates, fragrant bread, and savory meats are arranged artfully, as if placed by an unseen hand.

The composition is warm and inviting, with a sense of wonder and amazement. The warm earth tones, with accents of deep green and golden brown, dominate the color palette, evoking a sense of comfort and tranquility. The artist's brushstrokes are soft and expressive, imbuing the scene with a sense of watercolor magic.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.
